skill.name: observability-monitoring-monitor-setup
skill.description: Guides setup of observability systems with metrics, logs, traces, dashboards, alerts, SLOs, and monitoring infrastructure examples.
skill.summary: Create monitoring and observability plans for services, Kubernetes workloads, and production operations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, weak cryptography, network, and credential-access patterns. Review found the command and weak-crypto findings are false positives from Markdown code fences, template strings, and monitoring examples, with no executable skill scripts or prompt injection found. The remaining risk is medium because copied observability examples read environment variables, tail container logs, and send traces or dashboard traffic to configured endpoints.

medium_findings.title: Observability examples read runtime configuration and forward telemetry
medium_findings.description: The playbook includes examples that read environment variables for telemetry, Kubernetes, Elasticsearch, Slack, and PagerDuty configuration. This is expected for monitoring setup, but copied configurations can expose traces, logs, or alert credentials if endpoints and secrets are not controlled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment-variable access and telemetry destinations are explicit in the examples. The usage is legitimate for observability, so the concern is operational misuse rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Log aggregation examples access host log paths
medium_findings.description: The Fluentd example tails container logs and buffers data under local log directories. This is normal for cluster logging, but users should restrict collected fields and file permissions to avoid accidental secret collection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are explicit and semantically tied to log aggregation. The risk depends on deployment context because the skill does not execute these configurations itself.

low_findings.title: False-positive command execution detections
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick detections occur in Markdown code fences, TypeScript template strings, Prometheus expressions, and resource links. No evidence found that the skill executes shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are documentation syntax or inert examples. The reviewed files contain no executable script entrypoint that would run these strings.

low_findings.title: False-positive weak cryptography detections
low_findings.description: The flagged weak-cryptography locations are OpenTelemetry imports, alert template text, dashboard output headings, and monitoring prose. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, or other weak cryptographic algorithms being used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines did not find cryptographic APIs or digest algorithms. The static pattern appears to match unrelated words and template syntax.

low_findings.title: Critical combined heuristic dismissed after semantic review
low_findings.description: The scanner combined code, network, and credential-access signals into a critical heuristic. No evidence found of malicious intent, data exfiltration logic, obfuscation, or prompt injection in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The underlying signals are real documentation examples, but the reviewed skill lacks executable code or instructions to hide or exfiltrate data. The confidence is high but not perfect because users may copy configurations into sensitive environments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment-configured telemetry destinations
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples allow telemetry, log, and alert destinations to be supplied from environment variables or infrastructure variables. Users should verify endpoints and secret handling before deployment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly visible in the configuration examples and is security-relevant for deployment. It is not inherently malicious because this is normal observability configuration.

content.value_statement: Teams often lack consistent monitoring across metrics, logs, traces, alerts, and SLOs. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design practical observability setups with implementation checklists.
actual_capabilities: Designs monitoring architecture across metrics, logs, traces, dashboards, alerts, and SLOs.
Provides Prometheus scrape, alert rule, and recording rule patterns.
Outlines Grafana dashboard panels for request rate, errors, and latency percentiles.
Shows OpenTelemetry tracing setup concepts for service instrumentation.
Covers Fluentd-style log aggregation and structured logging patterns.
Includes Terraform-oriented monitoring module examples and alert routing guidance.
limitations: Does not deploy infrastructure or validate live cluster permissions by itself.
Examples require adaptation for each cloud, Kubernetes, and security environment.
Does not replace vendor documentation for Prometheus, Grafana, OpenTelemetry, or Fluentd.
Alert thresholds and SLO targets must be tuned with production traffic data.

use_cases.title: Create a Service Monitoring Baseline
use_cases.description: Define core metrics, traces, logs, dashboards, alerts, and runbooks for a new production service.

use_cases.title: Improve Incident Visibility
use_cases.description: Review gaps in current telemetry and create dashboards that reduce investigation time during incidents.

use_cases.title: Plan Observable Application Releases
use_cases.description: Add instrumentation and SLO checks before launching a feature or migrating a service.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Current Monitoring
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner assessment for an existing service.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current service monitoring setup. Identify missing metrics, logs, traces, dashboards, alerts, and SLOs. Keep the plan practical for a small team.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Monitoring Stack
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an observability stack for a Kubernetes service using Prometheus, Grafana, OpenTelemetry, and centralized logging. Include deployment steps and validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Create Alerts and Runbooks
prompt_templates.prompt: Create alert rules, severities, notification routes, and response runbooks for latency, error rate, saturation, and dependency failures in this service.

prompt_templates.title: Build an SLO Program
prompt_templates.prompt: Define SLOs, error budgets, burn rate alerts, dashboards, and review workflows for a multi-service platform with production and staging environments.

output_examples.input: A team asks for monitoring for a new API service.
output: A short infrastructure assessment covering current telemetry gaps.
A monitoring architecture with metrics, logs, traces, dashboards, and alerts.
A phased implementation checklist with validation steps and owners.

output_examples.input: An SRE wants better incident dashboards.
output: Dashboard sections for request rate, latency, errors, saturation, and dependencies.
Suggested alert thresholds linked to user impact and service objectives.
Runbook notes for triage, escalation, and post-incident review.

output_examples.input: A platform team wants SLO coverage for several services.
output: Recommended availability and latency objectives by service tier.
Burn rate alert strategy for fast and slow budget consumption.
Governance steps for reviews, exceptions, and continuous tuning.

best_practices: Start with user-facing golden signals before adding low-level infrastructure metrics.
Treat alert rules as production code with review, testing, ownership, and runbooks.
Redact secrets from logs and traces before sending telemetry to shared systems.
anti_patterns: Creating dashboards without clear service ownership or incident workflows.
Alerting on every anomaly instead of actionable symptoms and SLO burn rates.
Copying example endpoints, secrets, or file paths into production without review.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create monitoring plans, dashboard designs, alert strategies, SLO definitions, and instrumentation guidance.

faq.question: Does it install Prometheus or Grafana automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that users must adapt, review, and deploy in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it help with OpenTelemetry?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes tracing setup concepts and service instrumentation guidance using OpenTelemetry patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe for production systems?
faq.answer: Use it as planning guidance. Review endpoints, permissions, log contents, and secret handling before production deployment.

faq.question: Which teams benefit most from it?
faq.answer: Platform, SRE, DevOps, and application teams that need consistent service observability can benefit from it.

faq.question: Does it define alert thresholds automatically?
faq.answer: It suggests patterns, but teams must tune thresholds with real traffic, historical incidents, and business objectives.
